title: Summer cooling strategies for outdoor pigs
subtitle: Heat stress is the leading cause of summer pig losses in the UK.
excerpt: Pigs do not sweat. In the UK summer they can lose condition in a single hot afternoon and die in a heatwave if the wallow is dry and the shade is thin. This covers the wallow rebuild, the shade calculation, the water flow rate, and the early-warning signs that tell you a pig is overheating before she goes down.
sourceNotes: AHDB Pork outdoor production heat-stress guidance. DEFRA welfare code for pigs, environmental enrichment chapter. Cornell Small Farms outdoor pig hot-weather management notes.

glossaryTerms.definition: A shallow mud bath the pigs make themselves once a damp hollow is provided. The water cools the skin and the mud coat that dries afterwards is an effective sunblock and fly repellent.

How an animal controls its core temperature. Pigs lack functional sweat glands and rely on behaviour (wallowing, panting, lying on cool ground) rather than the sweating that does the work in sheep, cattle, and humans.

recipeTools.notes: Sited so the door faces away from the strongest afternoon sun.

content.text: A pig running a high core temperature on a hot July afternoon is in trouble inside two hours. The cooling kit on a smallholding is simple: a working
wallow

, enough shade to fit the whole group at once, and a water flow rate that keeps the trough cool through the day. Getting these three right turns a heatwave into an inconvenience. Getting them wrong turns it into a vet call.
Why pigs feel heat differently
Pig
thermoregulation

works through behaviour rather than skin cooling. With no functional sweat glands, a pig dumps heat by lying on cool ground, panting, and coating itself in water and mud. A black or red-skinned pig also sunburns; a sunburnt pig is then reluctant to lie down on hot soil, which loops the problem. The threshold to watch in the UK is about twenty-two degrees ambient with sun. Above twenty-six, every pig on the holding is at risk.
The wallow

A working wallow is a shallow hollow about two metres across and twenty to thirty centimetres deep at the centre, kept topped up with fresh water. Dig the hollow in the lowest corner of the paddock that will hold water, line the deepest part with a square of butyl pond liner if the ground is too free-draining, and let the pigs do the rest. They will work the mud, deepen the hollow over a week, and use it as their primary cooling station from May through September.

Top up the wallow with the hose every morning during a heatwave. The water level wants to be at least belly-deep on the pig; deeper than that wastes water and undermines the bank. A wallow that has dried out by noon is no use; check it at first light and refill before the heat builds.
Shade calculation

Every pig in the group needs to fit in the shade at the same time, with room to lie flat on the cool side without piling. A weaner needs about a quarter of a square metre; a finisher needs around three quarters of a square metre; a sow needs a clear two metres. Pig arcs are the standard shade structure on UK holdings, but a single arc rarely shades enough ground in the afternoon. Add a tarp slung between two posts on the south side of the paddock so the shade follows the sun through the day.

Trees are the best shade if you have them. A mature oak or sweet chestnut throws cool dense shade with airflow underneath, which is far more effective than a metal arc on baked ground. Site arcs so the door faces away from the south and west; the afternoon sun on the open door turns the inside into a sweat box.
Water

A finishing pig will drink eight to ten litres a day in normal weather and double that on a hot day. The trough wants to be in shade so the water itself stays cool; a black trough in full sun heats to a temperature the pigs refuse. A nipple drinker is the most reliable summer option because the water comes out cool from the supply and the pig drinks only what she wants. Check the flow rate weekly: dirt blocks the nipples slowly and a hungry mob can drain a slow drinker faster than it refills.
Spotting heat stress early

A pig that is too hot pants with her mouth open and breathes from the belly rather than the ribs. She moves slowly, separates from the group, and looks for the coolest patch of ground she can find. Skin on the ears and back goes a deeper red than usual. A pig down on her side and not getting up when called is past the early stage and needs cooling now: hose her flanks, get her into the wallow if she will move, and call the vet if she does not get up within twenty minutes.
The morning and evening rhythm

Pigs feed and root in the cool of the morning and the evening when the weather is hot. Shift the feeding routine to first light and last light during a heatwave; pigs that root through the heat of the day waste energy they should be putting into growth. A handful of cold apples or melon rind tossed into the wallow at midday is welcome and harmless.
What you might have found

attrs.heading: Summer pig troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Most summer problems are spotted early in the morning check.

items.symptom: Wallow has dried to crust by mid-morning.
items.cause: Free-draining ground, leak in the liner, or the pigs have pushed the bank out.
items.fix: Hose top-up that morning. Drop a butyl square in the deepest spot if the ground drains too fast. Rebuild the bank in the cool of the evening.

items.symptom: Pig with pink shoulders, peeling skin, and reluctance to lie on hot ground.
items.cause: Sunburn on a light-coloured pig with thin shade or short days of wallow use.
items.fix: Extend the shade with a tarp by lunchtime. Coat the affected pig with a layer of fresh mud from the wallow; the dried mud is the natural sunblock. Future-proof with a tarp before the next hot spell.

items.symptom: Trough water hot to the touch by midday.
items.cause: Trough sited in full sun, or the supply pipe runs along a hot wall.
items.fix: Move the trough into shade or sling a hessian sack over it kept wet through the day. A nipple drinker fed from a buried supply pipe stays cool and is the long-term answer.

items.symptom: A finisher pig lying flat, panting heavily, not getting up.
items.cause: Heat stress past the early stage. Core temperature is now above the safe range.
items.fix: Hose her flanks with cool water, not freezing cold. Help her into the wallow if she will move at all. Call the vet if she has not stood within twenty minutes. Do not move stock during a heatwave for any reason that can wait.

content.text: A holding set up well for summer reads quiet through August. Pigs lie in the wallow at midday, work the cool morning ground for roots, and put condition on through the season. Heat losses on a UK smallholding are nearly always a kit problem, not a pig problem. Build the wallow before May. Check the shade footprint on the longest day. Watch the trough flow in the first hot week.
